coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminometric fingerprints for identification of plasmonic nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shahrajabian, Maryam
Hormozi-Nezhad, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: Development of a convenient and inexpensive method for identification and detection of nanoparticles (NPs) is of great interest. In this work, we have developed a novel and simple chemiluminescence based sensor array, with its sensing mechanism mimicking that of olfactory and gustatory systems for discriminating a set of NPs. The proposed method is based on the enhancement effect of NPs on luminol–oxidant CL intensity by their catalytic effect. Three kinds of oxidant including H2O2, AgNO3, and K3Fe(CN)6 were used as sensor elements and NPs exhibited diverse enhancing responses to different oxidant-luminol CL systems producing unique response patterns that were identified through heat map and chemometric methods, including linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA). Five NPs have been well distinguished at various concentrations. In addition, this method clearly revealed a linear relationship between CL signal values and the concentrations of NPs for the quantitative detection of NPs. We believe that this type of CL sensor array can open a new way for facile discrimination and detection of different kinds of NPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Sensor array
Luminol–oxidant CL reaction
Enhancement effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Procedure of NPs Detection and Discrimination
Enhancement Effect of NPs on Luminol – Various Oxidant CL Reaction
Chemiluminometric Array-based Sensing System for NPs Discrimination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of nanoparticles. (a) CTAB-AuNPs, (b) Cit-AgNPs, (c) Cit-AuNPs, and (d) CTAB-AuNRs.
Number size distribution of the nanoparticles. (a) CTAB-AuNPs, (b) Cit-AuNPs, (c) Cit-AgNPs, and (d) Cyst-AgNPs.
Structure of flow injection chemiluminescence instrument.
(a) CL signals of different nanoparticles enhanced CL systems (b) Fingerprints for five selected NPs obtained from the ΔI patterns.
LDA plots of five NPs at different concentrations: (a) C, (b) C/2, (c) C/4, (d) C/5, and (e) C/10.
HCA of sensor repsonses for five kinds of NPs at various concentrations: (a) C, (b) C/2, (c) C/4, (d) C/5, and (e) C/10.
Heatmaps obtained from enhanced CL responses for five kinds of NPs at different concentrations: (a) C, (b) C/2, (c) C/4, (d) C/5, and (e) C/10.
(a) LDA, (b) HCA, (c) heat map of five NPs at same concentrations (0.5 nM).
Calibration curves of different NPs based on ΔI values of the luminol–oxidants CL systems.
LDA plots of five NPs and their mixture in tap water. (NPs concentration is C/10).

head.ce:title: Chemiluminometric fingerprints for identification of plasmonic nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran 11155-9516, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of a convenient and inexpensive method for identification and detection of nanoparticles (NPs) is of great interest. In this work, we have developed a novel and simple chemiluminescence based sensor array, with its sensing mechanism mimicking that of olfactory and gustatory systems for discriminating a set of NPs. The proposed method is based on the enhancement effect of NPs on luminol–oxidant CL intensity by their catalytic effect. Three kinds of oxidant including H2O2, AgNO3, and K3Fe(CN)6 were used as sensor elements and NPs exhibited diverse enhancing responses to different oxidant-luminol CL systems producing unique response patterns that were identified through heat map and chemometric methods, including linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA). Five NPs have been well distinguished at various concentrations. In addition, this method clearly revealed a linear relationship between CL signal values and the concentrations of NPs for the quantitative detection of NPs. We believe that this type of CL sensor array can open a new way for facile discrimination and detection of different kinds of NPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Today with the emergent and fast development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials have been successfully employed to improve the quality of life. Nanomaterials are known for their remarkable and novel properties such as optical and catalytic properties which distinguishes them dramatically from bulk materials. Consequently, Nanoparticles (NPs) are increasingly being used in various applications such as electronics [1], physics [2,3], information technology, sensors [4–6], drug delivery [7], healthcare and biomedicine [8]. Due to the crucial role of properties of NPs in their behavior, convenient methods for detection and identification of NPs are highly desirable.

Traditional analytical techniques, such as TEM, AFM, SEM, and X-ray-based techniques can provide accurate measurements of NPs, but their applications are hampered because of their time-consuming, costly and sophisticated instruments, complex preliminary treatment, and highly trained technicians, which are not suitable for simple and low-cost analysis. In addition, identification of NPs is possible by using a set of mentioned techniques, and by using just one of them it's not possible to characterize NPs with all of their physicochemical properties.

To overcome these drawbacks, an array sensing method provides an alternative choice. In a sensor array, a number of nonspecific sensor elements generate a unique set of responses for a special analyte, producing a pattern as a fingerprint for identification, that is similar to the olfactory systems or mammalian gustatory [9–12]. The sensor array technique has shown many advantages, such as diversity, accuracy, and ability to the simultaneous detection and discrimination of analytes with similar properties [11], thus it has been gotten much attention and broad successful applications in recent years. In the optical array based discrimination methods, different kinds of signals including absorption, fluorescence, and chemiluminescence (CL) can be employed [13]. Because of their high sensitivity, simple and cheaper instrumentation, ease of control and simple operation, our aim is to develop application of CL systems. The catalytic effect of NPs on CL reactions is well-known [14–22].

In recent years, the catalytic effect of NPs on chemiluminescence (CL) reactions and their CL signal amplification has opened a new window to the application of CL as powerful sensors and has attracted many researchers. Various nanoparticles, such as semiconductors, metals, metal oxides were used for different CL systems and have been widely employed in different detections. For example, Cui's group has reported that metal nanoparticles could catalyzed the chemiluminescent reactions including luminol-AgNO3 [23], luminol-K3Fe(CN)6 [24], luminol-H2O2 [25], lucigenin-KI [26], lucigenin-hydrazine [27], KIO4-NaOH/Na2CO3 [28], and lucigenin-ethanol [29]. These investigations have demonstrated that using metal nanoparticles in CL reactions could expand the application of CL sensors. Besides; different CL-based assays has been investigated by many research groups for the discrimination of many analytes according to the enhancement effect of NPs on CL reactions [30,31]. However, as far as we know, this strategy has not been yet reported for the discrimination of the own NPs. Therefore, in the present work, we introduce the first example of a CL array approach for the identification and detection of NPs. Two sensor array methods based on UV–Vis spectra for the discrimination of NPs was proposed by Hormozi Nezhah et al. [32,33]. However, the detection of NPs using a CL-based array has not been presented yet. In addition discrimination of NPs and some of their mixture in tap water was achieved by means of just three sensor elements as we know fewer sensor elements in a sensor array system is considered an advantage. Three kinds of oxidants including H2O2, AgNO3, K3Fe(CN)6 were employed as sensor elements for efficient detection and identification of NPs. Different CL response pattern for NPs was successfully achieved based on different enhancement effect of NPs on the three kinds of oxidants-luminol CL reactions. The CL patterns were constructed and transformed into canonical scores by means of linear discrimination analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), permitting a clear discrimination between NPs. Moreover, we demonstrated rapid visual identification of five NPs with the heat map, which can be easily shown specific patterns. Furthermore, our method was shown the good result to quantify the concentration of NPs.

Luminol (C8H7N3 95%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), potassium ferricyanide, sodium hydroxide, sodium borohydride (98%) and sodium citrate were bought from Merck. Ascorbic acid, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4·3H2O (99.5%)), hydrochloric acid, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), silver nitrate (AgNO3), 5‑bromosalicylic acid (5‑Br‑SA) and cysteamine (95%) were bought from Sigma. DI water (18.2 MΩ·cm, 25 °C) applied throughout all the experiments.

Our self-assembled flow injection CL instrument was used in this study. By using a Zetasizer Viscotec 802, NPs size distributions were obtained from Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS). The TEM images were obtained from a PHILIPS MC 10 TH microscope at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV.

Cit-AgNPs were synthesized according to the procedure described previously [34]. 250 μL 100 mM AgNO3 was added, with vigorous stirring, to ultrapure water (100 mL). Then, 250 μL sodium citrate (0.1 M) and 1 mL NaBH4 (5 mM) were added one after another. The mixture was stirred for half an hour and the resulting solution was put to the dark place for one day before utilize. Characterization of Cit-AgNPs is seen in Figs. 1 and 2 .

Cysteamine functionalized AgNPs (Cyst-AgNPs) were prepared based on previous report [35]. Typically, by adding 1.7 mg AgNO3 in 100 mL of DI water at room temperature a solution was prepared. Then it chemically reduced by 500 μL fresh prepared NaBH4 (4 mM). After reducing of AgNO3 with NaBH4, 500 mL of 3.9 mM cysteamine solution were added and it was placed under continuous stirring for 2 h. Characterization of AuNPs is seen in Fig. 2.

Cit-AuNPs were prepared according to Turkevich method. In brief, 50 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4 solution was boiled with vigorous stirring and then 5 mL of 38.8 mM sodium citrate was added. The solution was refluxed for 30 min and the boiling solution's color changed to red wine. Finally, ice-cold water was used for cooling prepared Au NPs and then stored at 4 °C temperature. Characterization of AuNPs is seen in Figs. 1 and 2.

CTAB–AuNPs were synthesized according to the procedure described previously [36,37]. Briefly, 600 mL, 0.01 M of a freshly prepared NaBH4 solution was added into the mixture solution of 250 mL, 0.01 M HAuCl4·3H2O and 10 mL, 0.1 M CTAB for preparation of seed solution and stirred for 20 min. The seed solution must be used within 2–5 h after preparation. The growth solution was prepared by addition of 500 μL 0.01 M HAuCl4·3H2O to 45 mL of 0.1 M CTAB. Then 100 mL of ascorbic acid (0.1 M) and 5 mL seed were added to15 mL of growth solution sequentially and stirred continuously for 10 min. Characterization of as-prepared nanospheres is seen in Figs. 1 and 2.

CTAB-AuNRs were prepared according to the procedure described previously [38]. Typically, 50 μL of 2.5 × 10−2 M HAuCl4 was added to the 5.0 mL of 0.1 M of CTAB under vigorous stirring and then 0.3 mL of 0.01 M fresh prepared NaBH4 was injected to this solution and stirred for 3 min for preparation of brownish yellow seed solution. The seed solution must be used within 2–5 h after preparation. 15 mg 5‑Br‑SA was added to 50 mL CTAB (0.05 M) under stirring and then 1.15 mL AgNO3 (0.01 M) was added. The mixture was stirred for 15 min, then 1 mL HAuCl4 (2.5 × 10−2 M), 400 μL of HCl (37 wt% in water) and 300 μL ascorbic acid (0.10) were added subsequently and finally, 600 μL of prepared seed solution was added under stirring. The solution was left overnight at room temperature and the solution's color changed from colorless to red. The synthesized Gold NRs were centrifuged two times (10,000 rpm, 10 min), and dissolved in DI water. Characterization of AuNRs is seen in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Procedure of NPs Detection and Discrimination
ce:section.ce:para: For discrimination of NPs, five kinds of NPs including Cit-AgNPs, Cyst-AgNPs, Cit-AuNPs, CTAB–AuNPs and CTAB-AuNRs were tested against the three kinds of oxidants. The NPs were injected into the fluid (DI water) via a loop-valve injector, followed by mixing with luminol solution (0.5 mM of luminol and 0.1 M NaOH), and oxidant including H2O2 (0.15 M) or K3Fe(CN)6 (0.73 μM) or AgNO3 (25 μM) through connectors. Finally, the mixture was transferred to a flow cell with the spiral shape that was set in a position just at the front part of a PMT and the CL signals were monitored. Three replicates were done for every one of NPs. The changes in CL response (ΔI=I − I0) were applied to detect of NPs and generate the pattern of responses, that I and I0 were the CL intensities of luminol-oxidant In presence and absence of NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement Effect of NPs on Luminol – Various Oxidant CL Reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic effect of NPs on various chemiluminescence reactions has been reported [14–20]. It was proved that NPs have the enhancement ability of luminol-hydrogen peroxide [39], luminol-silver nitrate [40] and luminol-potassium ferricyanide [41,42] reactions to produce strong CL emissions. For example, it has been widely studied, that the hydroxyl radicals are generated from hydrogen peroxide decomposition by NPs. Afterward hydroxyl radicals react with hydrogen peroxide to produce superoxide anions (O2
−) and luminol radicals. The strong CL signal is produced from the reaction between luminol radical and superoxide anion [39]. Generally, it is well-known that NPs have the enhancement effect on the luminol–oxidant CL reactions to generate strong CL radiation. In this study, we have demonstrated a new application of NPs enhanced CL system for discrimination of NPs themselves. The catalytic effect of various NPs on the luminol–H2O2, luminol–K3Fe(CN)6 and luminol–AgNO3 CL was examined via a flow injection CL instrument. As one can see in Fig. 3 , by means of an injector nanoparticles were injected in the fluid (DI water), blended with oxidant and luminol through connectors and flowed to a cell that was set in a position just at the front part of a PMT and the CL signals were obtained. Responses of various CL systems consisting three types of oxidants to various NPs are demonstrated in Fig. 4a. The results exhibited that the various NPs can cause significant different changes in the intensity of chemiluminescence emissions in the presence of three oxidants. These distinctive CL patterns indicate the feasibility of nanoparticles discrimination via a novel sensor array by utilizing three kinds of oxidants as sensing elements. Every kind of NP displays the special degree of enhancement on three oxidants-luminol CL systems and produces distinct response pattern as a fingerprint (Fig. 4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemiluminometric Array-based Sensing System for NPs Discrimination

ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the quantitative detection of NPs, the CL responses induced by luminol-oxidants at different concentrations of various NPs were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 9 , an obvious increase of the CL values was observed as the concentration of NPs increased and clearly revealed a linear relationship between ΔI values and the concentrations of NPs. Higher NPs concentration led to more catalytic enhancement, which conducted to higher CL signal.

In order to assess the application of the proposed method, the designed approach was applied to the discrimination of NPs and their mixture in the tap water spiked by NPs in C/10 concentration. The results in Fig. 10 demonstrate satisfying discrimination.

In summary, we have designed a novel chemiluminescence sensor array for the discrimination of NPs. The proposed sensor array was based on the enhancement degree of CL signals of three oxidants (H2O2, AgNO3, and K3Fe(CN)6)-luminol CL systems in the presence of various NPs as model targets. The CL intensity was altered to varying degrees upon the interaction of NPs with different oxidant-luminol systems and thus distinct CL response patterns were created. By using chemometric methods, the developed array differentiated among five kinds of NPs at different concentrations. In this work, linear relationship between enhanced CL signal values and the concentrations of NPs for the quantitative detection of NPs were obtained. We believe that CL based sensor arrays can find important applications, including their use as a simple way to NPs recognition and detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of this research by the Sharif University of Technology Research Council is gratefully acknowledged.
